definition: The ratio of potential profit to potential loss on a trade, measured in R multiples where 1R equals the stop-loss distance. A minimum 2R target is typically required, meaning the profit objective is at least twice the risk. The ratio improves by entering closer to invalidation, tightening stops relative to targets. It serves as the primary filter for trade viability, ensuring profitability with a sufficient win rate.
identification_rules: Define 1R as the distance from the entry price to the stop-loss level, with the stop placed beyond the invalidation point (protected swing, swept high/low, order block body, mean threshold, breaker block, etc.).
Define the reward as the distance from entry to a logical liquidity target (previous day high/low, swing highs/lows, standard deviation projection, fair value gap, opposite range extreme).
Calculate the risk-to-reward ratio as reward distance ÷ stop distance; the setup is valid only if the ratio is at least 2:1 (2R or greater).

When the initial stop placement yields less than 2R, tighten the stop to the mean threshold (0.5 Fib of the invalidation body) or relocate the entry to a tighter PD array (OTE zone, fair value gap, breaker block) to improve the ratio.
If the protected swing stop is too wide to reach 2R to the target, wait for a new protected swing or continuation order block to form closer to price before entering.
Ensure the 2R target is achievable before price encounters opposing higher-time-frame structure; if the next liquidity objective is too close, skip or defer the trade.
For scaling beyond 2R, project standard deviation levels (e.g., -2, -2.5) or hold runners toward larger structural objectives.
Maintain consistent dollar or percentage risk per trade to preserve the R multiple framework across trades.
Place a partial take-profit order to close half the position when the price moves to a 1:2 risk-to-reward level, letting the remainder run to the next key structural level.
When the zone (order block, supply/demand) is large, adjust entry to the middle or use a smaller sub-zone for the stop to improve the risk-to-reward ratio.
If extending a zone by including additional candles/wicks reduces the risk-to-reward ratio, switch to a lower timeframe for precise entry when price reaches the zone.

In an uptrend, confirm entry in the discount zone (below 50% Fibonacci retracement); in a downtrend, confirm entry in the premium zone (above 50% Fibonacci retracement) to maximize reward relative to risk.
Ensure the take-profit target is at a significant structural level (e.g., recent swing high/low) to maintain a realistic reward distance.
Never risk more than 2% of total trading capital on any single trade.
Limit active trades to a maximum of three at any one time to control overall exposure.
trading_implication: Risk-to-reward is the primary gate for trade entries. Each setup must offer at least 2R from a protected-swing stop to a defined liquidity target. When 2R is unavailable, traders refine entries to tighter PD arrays or wait for continuation structures to improve the ratio. Partial profits at 2R and runners toward extended liquidity targets scale returns while locking in the risk framework. Consistent application paired with fixed-dollar risk allows profitability even with a sub-50% win rate (e.g., >34%).
common_mistakes: Taking entries with stops on far swings instead of waiting for a closer protected swing or continuation entry.
Catching reversals rather than waiting for a continuation with tighter invalidation.
Using fixed contract size instead of fixed dollar/percentage risk, causing inconsistent R values.
Pushing entry too close to the stop to inflate R:R, resulting in missed fills or premature stop-outs.
Taking setups yielding less than 2R when a deeper PD array could improve the ratio.
Placing stops too tight on the body/mean threshold to boost R:R, leading to stop-outs on normal retests.
Placing stops at swing extremes instead of beyond structural levels like breaker blocks, reducing efficiency.
Entering without confirming a logical 2R target (e.g., liquidity pool) exists.
Chasing entries after large moves when insufficient room remains for 2R.
Assuming a favorable R:R guarantees a winning trade.
Closing the entire position at 2R without leaving runners for larger objectives.
Using a generic stop without adjusting for instrument-specific behavior (e.g., equal-high sweeps).
Entering at equilibrium (1:1) or on the wrong side of premium/discount.
Failing to update the trading range as price expands, leading to stale R:R calculations.
Adjusting the stop rather than the entry to improve R:R.
Not verifying higher-time-frame bias alignment with the 2R target.
Entering trades without a favorable risk-to-reward ratio (worse than 1:2), reducing long-term profitability.
Entering in the wrong zone (buying in premium or selling in discount), leading to a poor risk-to-reward ratio.
Not adjusting the stop-loss or entry point when the order block or zone is large, resulting in an unfavorable risk-to-reward ratio.
Failing to take partial profits at the 1:2 target, leaving gains exposed to reversal.
Failing to adjust the reward target to a meaningful structural level, resulting in an overestimated reward and unrealistic expectations.
Risking more than 2% of capital on a single trade, exposing the account to excessive drawdown.
Marking zones too narrowly without considering risk-to-reward, leading to missed trades in trending markets.
Expanding zones without checking if the risk-to-reward ratio remains viable, resulting in poor trade outcomes.
merge_notes: All three input cards agree on school (Risk Management). No school conflict. The SMC card uses 1:2 minimum while ICT cards use 2R; both are equivalent. The SMC card adds partial profit at 1:2 and 2% risk rule, which are preserved. No major disagreements.
